concepts.definition: The delivery of computing services (compute, storage, databases, networking, software, analytics) over the internet ('the cloud') on a pay-as-you-go basis, provisioned on-demand from a shared pool of resources.
concepts.details: Instead of buying and maintaining physical servers in your own datacenter, you rent capacity from a provider like Microsoft, Amazon, or Google. The provider owns and maintains the physical hardware; you consume it as a service.
examTips: AZ-900 tests conceptual understanding, not deployment skill — know the 'why' behind cloud adoption, not just the 'what'.
Know the difference between 'consumption-based' pricing and traditional licensing.
gotchas: Cloud computing is not just 'someone else's server' — the exam expects you to articulate the service model and economic shift (CapEx to OpEx), not just the hosting location.
concepts.plainEnglish: Think of electricity: you don't own a power plant, you just plug in and pay for what you use each month. Cloud computing works the same way for computers — Microsoft owns and runs giant datacenters full of servers, and you 'plug in' over the internet and pay only for the compute, storage, or software you actually consume.

quickCheck.question: True or false: with cloud computing, you must buy and own the physical servers you use.
quickCheck.answer: False. The cloud provider (e.g., Microsoft) owns and maintains the physical hardware. You rent access to it and pay based on usage.

concepts.definition: Cloud services offered over the public internet and shared across multiple organizations (tenants), owned and operated by a third-party provider (e.g., Azure, AWS, GCP).
concepts.details: No capital expenditure to build out infrastructure; the provider is responsible for hardware maintenance and datacenter management. Resources are typically available on-demand and can be self-serviced by customers.
examTips: Public cloud = lowest CapEx, highest scalability, shared infrastructure with strong isolation via virtualization.
gotchas: 'Public' refers to the availability model (open to any customer), not that your data is visible to others — tenants are logically isolated.
concepts.plainEnglish: Imagine an apartment building: everyone rents their own private unit, but the building, elevators, and land are owned by the landlord and shared across all tenants. Public cloud is like that — Azure owns the 'building' (datacenters), and many different customers rent space in it, safely walled off from each other.

quickCheck.question: In the public cloud model, who owns the physical hardware?
quickCheck.answer: The third-party provider (e.g., Microsoft), not the customer. Customers are tenants who rent capacity.

concepts.definition: A cloud environment dedicated entirely to a single organization, which may be hosted on-premises or by a third party but is not shared with other tenants.
concepts.details: Offers more control over security and compliance, but the organization (or its designated operator) is generally responsible for purchasing and maintaining hardware, resulting in ongoing CapEx.
examTips: Private cloud is preferred when regulatory/compliance requirements demand full control over hardware and data location.
gotchas: A private cloud can still use cloud-style tooling (self-service portals, automation) — 'private' describes tenancy, not lack of cloud characteristics.
concepts.plainEnglish: This is like owning a house instead of renting an apartment: nobody else lives there, you have full control over every room, but you're also the one who has to fix the roof and pay the property taxes. A private cloud gives one organization exclusive infrastructure, with more control but more responsibility.

quickCheck.question: A hospital needs full control over its infrastructure for strict regulatory reasons and doesn't want to share hardware with any other organization. Which model fits best?
quickCheck.answer: Private cloud — dedicated infrastructure for a single organization, giving maximum control.

concepts.definition: A computing environment that combines a public cloud and a private/on-premises environment, allowing data and applications to be shared between them.
concepts.details: Useful for organizations with legacy investments, data residency constraints, or a gradual cloud migration strategy. Azure Arc and Azure Stack are Microsoft's flagship hybrid tools.
examTips: Know Azure Arc: extends Azure management to resources running anywhere — on-prem, other clouds, edge.
Know Azure Stack: brings Azure services into your own datacenter.
gotchas: Hybrid is not just 'using two clouds' — it specifically implies integration/interoperability between public and private environments, not isolated use of both.
concepts.plainEnglish: Picture a business that keeps its most sensitive files in a locked office safe (on-premises) but stores everything else in a rented storage unit across town (public cloud) — and the two are connected so staff can move things between them as needed. That connection between 'your own stuff' and 'the public cloud' is hybrid cloud.

quickCheck.question: A company keeps its customer database on-premises for compliance reasons but runs its public website in Azure, with the two systems talking to each other. What is this called?
quickCheck.answer: Hybrid cloud — a mix of private/on-premises and public cloud resources that are integrated together.

concepts.definition: The use of cloud services from more than one public cloud provider (e.g., Azure + AWS).
concepts.details: Adopted for redundancy, avoiding vendor lock-in, or leveraging best-of-breed services from different providers. Azure Arc can also manage resources across multiple clouds.
examTips: The exam may distinguish multi-cloud (multiple public providers) from hybrid (public + private).
gotchas: Multi-cloud and hybrid cloud are not synonyms, though a deployment can be both simultaneously.
concepts.plainEnglish: This is like having accounts with two different rideshare apps so you're never stuck if one has no drivers nearby. A company using both Azure and AWS for different needs — or as a backup plan — is running multi-cloud.

quickCheck.question: A company runs some workloads in Azure and others in AWS to avoid depending on a single vendor. What is this strategy called?
quickCheck.answer: Multi-cloud — using more than one public cloud provider.

concepts.definition: The ability of a system to remain operational and accessible for a high percentage of time, minimizing downtime, typically expressed as an SLA percentage (e.g., 99.9%).
concepts.details: Azure achieves HA through redundancy: multiple instances across fault domains, availability zones, and paired regions.
examTips: Memorize how SLA 'nines' translate to downtime: 99.9% ≈ 8.7 hrs/year down, 99.99% ≈ 52.6 min/year down.
gotchas: High availability is about resilience to failure, not raw performance/speed — don't confuse with scalability.
concepts.plainEnglish: Think of a 24-hour convenience store with a backup generator — even if the power grid goes down, the lights stay on and customers can still shop. High availability means your application keeps running (or fails over instantly) even when something breaks, so users barely notice.

quickCheck.question: A service is guaranteed to be up 99.9% of the time. Roughly how much downtime per year does that allow?
quickCheck.answer: About 8.7 hours per year. Memorize: 99% ≈ 3.65 days/yr, 99.9% ≈ 8.7 hrs/yr, 99.95% ≈ 4.4 hrs/yr, 99.99% ≈ 52.6 min/yr.

concepts.definition: The ability to increase or decrease allocated resources to meet demand.
concepts.details: Two types: vertical scaling (scale up/down — changing the size/SKU of a resource, e.g., a bigger VM) and horizontal scaling (scale out/in — adding or removing instances, e.g., more VMs behind a load balancer).
examTips: Scale UP/DOWN = vertical (change resource size). Scale OUT/IN = horizontal (change instance count). This distinction is a classic exam question.
gotchas: Horizontal scaling generally provides better fault tolerance than vertical scaling because it avoids a single point of failure.
concepts.plainEnglish: Imagine a restaurant that can either upgrade to a bigger kitchen (vertical: scale up) or open more food trucks around town (horizontal: scale out) when it gets busier. Both are ways of scaling, but they work differently — one makes a single unit bigger, the other adds more units.

quickCheck.question: A team upgrades a single VM from 4 vCPUs to 16 vCPUs to handle more load. Is this scaling up/down or scaling out/in?
quickCheck.answer: Scaling up (vertical scaling) — increasing the size/power of a single resource, not adding more instances.

concepts.definition: The ability of a cloud system to automatically scale resources up or down based on real-time demand, without manual intervention.
concepts.details: Elasticity is scalability plus automation — think autoscale rules on a VM Scale Set that add instances during peak load and remove them afterward.
examTips: Elasticity is frequently paired with 'pay only for what you use' as a cost benefit.
gotchas: Elasticity implies automatic response to demand; manually resizing a VM is scalability, not elasticity.
concepts.plainEnglish: Think of a rubber band: it stretches when you pull it and snaps back when you let go, without you doing anything manual. Elasticity is that same automatic stretch-and-shrink behavior applied to cloud resources — more servers spin up during a big sale, then spin back down automatically once traffic drops.

quickCheck.question: What's the key difference between scalability and elasticity?
quickCheck.answer: Scalability is the ability to add/remove resources at all. Elasticity specifically means that scaling happens automatically in response to real-time demand, in both directions.

concepts.definition: The speed at which cloud resources can be provisioned, configured, and deployed, enabling faster experimentation and time-to-market.
concepts.details: Spinning up a new VM or database in minutes (vs. weeks of procurement on-prem) is the classic example of cloud agility.
gotchas: Agility refers to speed of provisioning/deployment, distinct from elasticity (automated scaling) and fault tolerance (resilience).
concepts.plainEnglish: In the old days, ordering a new server could mean weeks of waiting for a purchase order, shipping, and setup. In the cloud, you can spin up a new server in minutes with a few clicks. That speed — from idea to running system — is agility.

quickCheck.question: A developer needs a new test environment and has it running within 10 minutes using the Azure Portal, instead of waiting weeks for procurement. Which cloud benefit does this demonstrate?
quickCheck.answer: Agility — the speed of provisioning and deploying resources.

concepts.definition: Fault tolerance is a system's ability to keep operating despite a component failure; disaster recovery is the strategy and tooling to restore operations after a major outage.
concepts.details: Azure Site Recovery (ASR) replicates workloads to a secondary region for DR. Fault domains and availability zones provide fault tolerance within a region.
examTips: Know Azure Site Recovery = DR tool; Azure Backup = data backup tool. They're commonly confused on the exam.
gotchas: Backup protects against data loss/corruption; DR protects against full site/region outages — they solve different problems even though they're related.
concepts.plainEnglish: Fault tolerance is like a car having a spare tire — a single flat doesn't strand you. Disaster recovery is more like having a whole spare car parked in another city in case your entire garage burns down. One handles small failures gracefully; the other handles a total catastrophe.

quickCheck.question: Which Azure service is specifically designed to replicate an entire workload to a secondary region for disaster recovery?
quickCheck.answer: Azure Site Recovery (ASR) — not Azure Backup, which protects against data loss/corruption rather than full-site outages.

concepts.definition: CapEx (Capital Expenditure) is upfront investment in physical infrastructure (owned assets, depreciated over time). OpEx (Operational Expenditure) is ongoing, pay-as-you-go spending for consumed services.
concepts.details: Cloud computing shifts organizations from CapEx (buying servers) to OpEx (renting compute/storage as needed), improving cash flow flexibility and reducing upfront risk.
examTips: This CapEx→OpEx shift is one of the most frequently tested concepts on AZ-900 — expect a direct question.
gotchas: OpEx isn't automatically 'cheaper' — it trades upfront cost for ongoing/variable cost; the exam tests understanding of the trade-off, not that OpEx always wins financially.
concepts.plainEnglish: Buying a car outright is CapEx — one big payment upfront, and then it's yours. Leasing a car with monthly payments based on how much you drive is more like OpEx — you pay over time, tied to usage, with less commitment. The cloud shifts IT spending from 'buy the car' to 'lease based on use.'

quickCheck.question: A company sells its on-premises servers and switches to paying Azure a monthly bill based on usage. Is this a shift toward CapEx or OpEx?
quickCheck.answer: OpEx (Operational Expenditure) — ongoing, usage-based spending instead of a large upfront capital purchase.

concepts.definition: A pricing model where customers pay only for the resources they actually use, rather than a fixed fee regardless of usage.
concepts.details: Also called 'pay-as-you-go.' Contrasts with traditional models where you pay for capacity whether you use it or not.
gotchas: Some Azure services (e.g., Reserved Instances) let you pre-commit for a discount — this is still cloud pricing but is not purely consumption-based.
concepts.plainEnglish: It's like a taxi meter instead of a monthly parking garage pass — you pay based on exactly how much you use, not a flat fee regardless of usage.

quickCheck.question: What is the main advantage of a consumption-based pricing model over a traditional fixed-license model?
quickCheck.answer: You only pay for what you actually use, rather than paying a fixed amount regardless of usage — this can significantly reduce waste and cost for variable workloads.

concepts.definition: The cost advantage a large-scale provider gets by buying hardware, power, and datacenter capacity in bulk — savings it can pass on to customers as lower per-unit prices than any single customer could achieve buying on their own.
concepts.details: Because Microsoft operates datacenters at a massive global scale, it negotiates better prices on hardware, electricity, and networking than an individual company ever could for its own on-prem datacenter. This is one of the core economic arguments for why cloud pricing can be cheaper than self-hosting, even after the provider takes a margin.
concepts.plainEnglish: It's the same reason a big-box warehouse store can sell a jar of peanut butter cheaper than your corner shop: buying in bulk drives the per-unit cost down. Microsoft buys server hardware and electricity at a scale no single company could match, and some of that savings gets passed down to you.
examTips: Economies of scale is the economic reasoning BEHIND why consumption-based cloud pricing can beat on-prem costs — the exam may ask you to identify this as the underlying principle in a cost-comparison scenario.
gotchas: Economies of scale explains why cloud CAN be cheaper — it doesn't guarantee every workload will be cheaper in the cloud; poorly optimized cloud usage can still cost more than efficient on-prem infrastructure.

quickCheck.question: Why can Microsoft often offer lower infrastructure costs than a company running its own datacenter?
quickCheck.answer: Economies of scale — Microsoft buys hardware, power, and capacity in bulk across a massive global footprint, driving down the per-unit cost in a way no single company could match on its own.

concepts.definition: The provider supplies raw compute, storage, and networking infrastructure; the customer manages the OS, runtime, and applications.
concepts.details: Example: Azure Virtual Machines. Gives maximum control and flexibility but requires the most management overhead (patching OS, installing software).
examTips: IaaS = 'rent the hardware, manage everything above it.' Classic example: Azure VMs, Azure Virtual Network.
gotchas: Even in IaaS, the underlying physical hardware, virtualization layer, and networking fabric are still the provider's responsibility (shared responsibility model).
concepts.plainEnglish: IaaS is like renting an empty apartment: the landlord (Microsoft) handles the building, plumbing, and electricity, but you bring your own furniture, decide the layout, and are responsible for keeping your own space clean. With IaaS (e.g., Azure VMs), Microsoft handles the physical hardware, but you install and manage the operating system and everything on top of it.

quickCheck.question: You deploy an Azure Virtual Machine and are responsible for installing the OS, applying security patches, and installing your application. What service model is this?
quickCheck.answer: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — you manage everything from the OS upward; the provider manages the physical infrastructure.

concepts.definition: The provider manages infrastructure and the runtime environment; the customer focuses only on deploying and managing their application/code.
concepts.details: Example: Azure App Service, Azure SQL Database. Reduces operational burden (no OS patching) while still allowing custom application code.
examTips: PaaS = 'bring your code, we handle the rest.' Classic examples: App Service, Azure SQL DB, Azure Functions runtime layer.
gotchas: PaaS customers still configure application settings and manage data — 'fully managed infrastructure' doesn't mean zero customer responsibility.
concepts.plainEnglish: PaaS is like renting a furnished apartment with maintenance included: you don't worry about plumbing or furniture, you just move in and live your life. With PaaS (e.g., Azure App Service), Microsoft manages the OS and runtime — you just deploy your application code.

quickCheck.question: A developer deploys code to Azure App Service and never has to think about OS patching or server maintenance. What service model is this?
quickCheck.answer: PaaS (Platform as a Service) — the provider manages the OS and runtime; the customer manages the application and data.

concepts.definition: The provider hosts and manages the entire application stack; the customer simply uses the software, typically via a browser.
concepts.details: Example: Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365, Outlook.com. The customer's responsibility is largely limited to data, user access, and device management.
examTips: SaaS = 'just use the app.' Classic example the exam loves: Microsoft 365 / Exchange Online.
gotchas: SaaS customers are still responsible for their own data and identity/access management — that never fully transfers to the provider under the shared responsibility model.
concepts.plainEnglish: SaaS is like staying at a hotel: you don't own or maintain anything — you just show up and use the room, bed, and Wi-Fi someone else set up and maintains. Using Outlook.com or Microsoft 365 is SaaS — you just use the finished product.

quickCheck.question: Which service model requires the least management effort from the customer?
quickCheck.answer: SaaS (Software as a Service) — the provider manages the entire stack, including the application; the customer just uses it.

concepts.definition: A framework defining which security and management tasks are handled by the cloud provider versus the customer, and how that split shifts depending on service type (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS).
concepts.details: Moving from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS shifts more responsibility to Microsoft. Data, endpoints, account/identities, and access management remain the customer's responsibility in ALL models.
examTips: Memorize the shifting boundary: On-Prem (customer manages everything) → IaaS → PaaS → SaaS (Microsoft manages progressively more).
The customer ALWAYS retains responsibility for data classification, identities, and endpoint devices, regardless of service model.
gotchas: This is one of the most commonly missed exam topics — remember the physical datacenter/host/network layers move to the provider first, but data and identity NEVER fully transfer.
concepts.plainEnglish: Think of renting a storage unit: the facility guarantees the building is secure and the roof doesn't leak (their responsibility), but YOU are always responsible for locking your own unit and deciding what to store in it (your responsibility) — no matter which storage plan you pick. In the cloud, your data, devices, and accounts are always your job; the provider takes on more of the rest as you move from IaaS to PaaS to SaaS.

quickCheck.question: Under the shared responsibility model, which single item is the customer responsible for in EVERY service model — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS?
quickCheck.answer: Data (along with identities, devices, and access management) — these never fully transfer to the provider, regardless of service model.

concepts.definition: A cloud execution model where the provider dynamically manages the allocation of compute resources, and billing is based on actual execution rather than pre-allocated capacity.
concepts.details: Servers still exist — 'serverless' means the customer doesn't provision or manage them. Azure Functions is the primary example.
examTips: Serverless is a subtype of PaaS — know Azure Functions as the flagship example.
gotchas: Serverless does not mean 'no servers exist' — it means the customer has zero visibility into or management responsibility for the underlying servers.
concepts.plainEnglish: It's like using a vending machine instead of running your own snack shop: you don't manage the machine, stock, or building — you just press a button and get your snack, paying only for what you take. With serverless (Azure Functions), you write a small piece of code, and it only runs — and you only pay — when it's actually triggered.

quickCheck.question: Does 'serverless' mean there are no servers involved at all?
quickCheck.answer: No — servers still exist and run the code. 'Serverless' means the customer has no visibility into or responsibility for managing those servers.
